topic: Alloc - Box<T>: Ownership, Coercions, and Into Raw

turns.content: I'm used to Java where everything is boxed on the heap automatically. I want to use Box<dyn Trait> in my Rust open-source project for type erasure, but I keep getting confused about ownership and lifetimes.

You're right that Java boxes things implicitly, but in Rust you must explicitly create a Box<T> to move the value onto the heap. When you write Box<dyn Trait>, you are telling the compiler to store a dynamically typed pointer on the heap so it can hold any type implementing that trait without knowing the concrete type at compile time.
So I just need to make sure my function accepts Box<dyn Trait> instead of &dyn Trait, right? And since Java doesn't have this distinction, maybe I can ignore ownership rules?

No, you cannot ignore Rust's ownership rules. If your parameter is Box<dyn Trait>, the caller must give you a moved value that owns the heap allocation. A reference &dyn Trait does not transfer ownership; it only borrows data from somewhere else. In Java you might pass an object around freely because the JVM manages references, but in Rust, if you move a Box into your function and then try to use its contents after returning, you'll get a compilation error unless you explicitly clone or drop the interior value.
Got it. But what about performance? Is there a big difference between using Box<T> versus just taking a &T reference in my code?

There is a significant memory and safety trade-off here. Taking &T means you only borrow the stack value, which has zero allocation cost but no ownership transfer. Box<T> always performs an allocation because it moves the value onto the heap. If your data fits in a register or small cache line, &T is faster and uses less memory, but if you need to pass ownership across function boundaries or threads, then Box<T> is required so the caller retains control over when the data gets dropped.
I see. I want to use this in an FFI layer where C code will send me a boxed struct. Should I use into_raw and from_raw instead of normal Box operations?

Yes, for FFI you typically avoid the extra indirection that normal Box<T> creates by using Box::into_raw to extract the raw pointer and then calling C functions directly with that pointer. You can later use Box::from_raw to recreate a safe Box<T> once control returns to Rust code. This avoids an intermediate heap allocation step, but you must ensure the raw pointer remains valid throughout its lifetime so it doesn't get freed prematurely.
One more thing I'm worried about: what if I accidentally move a Box out of a function and then try to use its contents again? Will the compiler catch that?

The compiler will absolutely catch double-free or reuse errors because Rust enforces strict ownership rules. If you move a Box<T> into a function, the original owner loses access to it immediately. Any attempt to borrow from the moved Box without re-obtaining ownership via clone() or another allocation path will fail at compile time. This is why Rust's safety model prevents common bugs that Java developers might encounter with manual memory management.
